confidence.disclosure: Shehu Usman dan Fodio (Uthman ibn Fudi), founder of the Sokoto Caliphate in West Africa, is documented in his own Arabic and Fulfulde writings, in the chronicles of his brother Abdullahi dan Fodio (Tazyin al-Waraqat) and his son Muhammad Bello (Infaq al-Maysur), and in oral traditions later collected by colonial-era scholars. His birth in Maratta in Gobir, his early teaching career, his break with the Hausa kings, the famous Hijra to Gudu in February 1804, the jihad campaigns, the founding of Sokoto, and his death in 1817 are firmly placed by year.

sources: Muhammad Bello, Infaq al-Maysur fi Tarikh Bilad al-Tukrur
Usman dan Fodio, Bayan Wujub al-Hijra
Mervyn Hiskett, The Sword of Truth: The Life and Times of the Shehu Usuman dan Fodio (1973)

journey.event: Born on 29 Safar 1168 AH (15 December 1754) in the village of Maratta in the Hausa kingdom of Gobir (modern northern Nigeria), to a learned Fulani family of the Toronkawa clan. His father Muhammadu Fodio is a Maliki scholar and teacher; his mother Hauwa is also of scholarly descent.

The family moves to Degel, a Fulani scholarly settlement in Gobir, where Usman is taught the Quran by his father and the Maliki legal texts by his uncles and cousins. Degel becomes his lifelong base and the heart of his early teaching network.

Around age 20 travels north into the Sahara to study with the famous Tuareg scholar Jibril ibn Umar at Agades. From Jibril he absorbs a rigorous reformist understanding of Islamic law and a strong concern for the moral state of West African Muslim societies. Jibril remains his lifelong inspiration.

Returns to Degel and begins his career as itinerant teacher and preacher. For the next quarter-century travels widely through the Hausa kingdoms of Gobir, Kebbi, and Zamfara, preaching to Muslims and pagans alike, calling for the strict observance of the sharia and condemning the syncretism of the Hausa courts.

Has a famous spiritual experience in which he sees the Prophet and is given the 'sword of truth' by Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani, the founder of the Qadiri Sufi order. Interprets the vision as a divine commission to revive Islam in the bilad al-sudan. From this point onward sees himself as a renewer (mujaddid) of his age.

Around 1794 is granted an audience with the Sarki (king) of Gobir, Bawa Jangwarzo, who under pressure from Usman's growing following grants the Muslim community in Gobir certain religious freedoms - the right to preach, to wear turbans, and to be exempt from certain taxes. The concession marks the high point of Usman's accommodationist phase.

Relations with the Hausa kings deteriorate sharply under the new Sarkin Gobir Yunfa, a former student of Usman who now sees the Shehu's growing community as a political threat. Yunfa attempts to assassinate Usman and orders his disciples to be expelled from Gobir.

On 10 Dhu al-Qa'da 1218 AH (21 February 1804), facing mounting persecution, Usman leads his community out of Degel to the small village of Gudu in conscious imitation of the Prophet's Hijra. At Gudu his followers proclaim him Amir al-Muminin (Commander of the Faithful) and pledge allegiance to him as leader of an Islamic state. The Sokoto jihad has begun.

On 21 June 1804 the army of Sarkin Gobir Yunfa attacks the Muslim camp at Lake Kwotto. Despite being heavily outnumbered, Usman's forces - led in the field by his brother Abdullahi - win a decisive victory. The battle of Tabkin Kwotto becomes the founding military event of the Sokoto Caliphate.

The jihad spreads rapidly across Hausaland as Fulani communities and disaffected Hausa peasants rally to Usman's cause. Flag-bearers are dispatched to other Hausa cities - Kano, Katsina, Zaria, Daura - each authorised to wage jihad and to establish a local emirate under Sokoto's overlordship.

In October 1808 the army of the jihad, led by Muhammad Bello, captures Alkalawa, the capital of Gobir. Sarkin Yunfa is killed in the fighting. The fall of Alkalawa marks the end of organised Hausa resistance and the consolidation of the new Sokoto Caliphate, which now controls most of Hausaland.

Establishes a new capital at Sokoto, founded by his son Muhammad Bello at the confluence of the Sokoto and Rima rivers. From Sokoto the caliphate is administered through a network of emirates stretching from modern Burkina Faso to Cameroon. Usman himself withdraws increasingly from politics to focus on writing and teaching.

Divides administrative authority between his brother Abdullahi (in charge of the western emirates from Gwandu) and his son Muhammad Bello (in charge of the eastern emirates from Sokoto). Withdraws to Sifawa to devote himself entirely to writing. Composes during these final years a series of major works on theology, law, Sufism, and political theory.

Returns to Sokoto in his final years. Continues to write and teach but his health declines. His wife Hauwa, the mother of Muhammad Bello, predeceases him. His daughter Nana Asma'u, herself a major poet and scholar, plays an increasingly prominent role in transmitting his teachings to the women of the caliphate.

Dies in Sokoto on 20 April 1817 (3 Jumada II 1232 AH), aged 63. Buried at the Hubbare in Sokoto, where his tomb remains the most important Muslim shrine in West Africa. His son Muhammad Bello succeeds him as second caliph. The Sokoto Caliphate he founded becomes the largest African state of the nineteenth century, lasting until the British conquest in 1903, and his religious legacy continues to shape Islam in northern Nigeria, Niger, and Cameroon to the present day.
